classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

title: Chapter XVIII.

source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: The judges said: We desire to have information from you on this point, Manichæus, to wit, to what effect you have affirmed him to be evil.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Do you mean that he has been so from the time when men were made, or before that period?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For it is necessary that you should give some proof of his wickedness from the very time from which you declare him to have been evil.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Be assured that the quality of a wine cannot be ascertained unless one first tastes it; and understand that, in like manner, every tree is known by its fruit.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: What say you, then?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: From what time has this personality been evil?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For an explanation of this problem seems to us to be necessary.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Manes said: He has always been so.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Archelaus said: Well, then, I shall also show from this, most excellent friends, and most judicious auditors, that his statement is by no means correct.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For iron, to take an example, has not been an evil thing always, but only from the period of man’s existence, and since his art turned it to evil by applying it to false uses; and every sin has come into existence since the period of man’s being.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Even that great serpent himself was not evil previous to man, but only after man, in whom he displayed the fruit of his wickedness, because he willed it himself.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: If, then, the father of wickedness makes his appearance to us after man has come into being, according to the Scriptures, how can he be unbegotten who has thus been constituted evil subsequently to man, who is himself a production?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But, again, why should he exhibit himself as evil just from the period when, on your supposition, he did himself create man?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: What did he desire in him?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: If man’s whole body was his own workmanship, what did he ardently affect in him?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For one who ardently affects or desires, desires something which is different and better.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: If, indeed, man takes his origin from him in respect of the evil nature, we see how man was his own, as I have frequently shown.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For if man was his own, he was also evil himself, just as it holds with our illustration of the like tree and the like fruit; for an evil tree, as you say, produces evil fruit.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And seeing that all were evil, what did he desiderate, or in what could he show the beginning of his wickedness, if from the time of man’s formation man was the cause of his wickedness?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Moreover, the law and precept having been given to the man himself, the man had not by any means the power to yield obedience to the serpent, and to the statements which were made by him; and had the man then yielded no obedience to him, what occasion would there have been for him to be evil?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But, again, if evil is unbegotten, how does it happen that man is sometimes found to be stronger than it?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For, by obeying the law of God, he will often overcome every root of wickedness; and it would be a ridiculous thing if he, who is but the production, should be found to be stronger than the unbegotten.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Moreover, whose is that law with its commandment—that commandment, I mean, which has been given to man?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Without doubt it will be acknowledged to be God’s.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And how, then, can the law be given to an alien? or who can give his commandment to an enemy?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Or, to speak of him who receives the commandment, how can he contend against the devil? that is to say, on this supposition, how can he contend against his own creator, as if the son, while he is a debtor to him for deeds of kindness, were to choose to inflict injuries on the father?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Thus you but mark out the profitlessness of man on this side, if you suppose him to be contradicting by the law and commandment him who has made him, and to be making the effort to get the better of him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Yea, we shall have to fancy the devil himself to have gone to such an excess of folly, as not to have perceived that in making man he made an adversary for himself, and neither to have considered what might be his future, nor to have foreseen the actual consequence of his act; whereas even in ourselves. who are but productions, there are at least some small gifts of knowledge, and a measure of prudence, and a moderate degree of consideration, which is sometimes of a very trustworthy nature.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And how, then, can we believe that in the unbegotten there is not some little portion of prudence, or consideration, or intelligence?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Or how can we make the contrary supposition, according to your assertion, namely, that he is discovered to be of the most senseless apprehension, and the dullest heart and in short rather like the brutes in his natural constitution?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But if the case stands thus, again, how is it that man, who is possessed of no insignificant power in mental capacity and knowledge, could have received his substance from one who thus is, of all beings, the most ignorant and the bluntest in apprehension?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: How shall any one be rash enough to profess that man is the workmanship of an author of this character?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But, again, if man consists both of soul and of body, and not merely of body without soul, and if the one cannot subsist apart from the other, why will you assert that these two are antagonistic and contrary to each other?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For our Lord Jesus Christ, indeed, seems to me to have spoken of these in His parables, when He said: “No man can put new wine into old bottles, else the bottles will break, and the wine run out.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But new wine is to be put into new bottles, as there is indeed one and the same Lord for the bottle and for the wine.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For although the substance may be different, yet by these two substances, in their due powers, and in the maintenance of their proper mutual relations, the one person of man subsists.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: We do not say, indeed, that the soul is of one substance with the body, but we aver that they have each their own characteristic qualities; and as the bottle and the wine are applied in the similitude to one race and one species of men, so truth’s reckoning requires us to grant that man was produced complete by the one God: for the soul rejoices in the body, and loves and cherishes it; and none the less does the body rejoice that it is quickened by the soul.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But if, on the other hand, a person maintains that the body is the work of the wicked one, inasmuch as it is so corruptible, and antiquated, and worthless, it would follow then that it is incapable of sustaining the virtue of the spirit or the movement of the soul, and the most splendid creation of the same.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For just as, when a person puts a piece of new cloth into an old garment, the rent is made worse; so also the body would perish if it were to be associated, under such conditions, with that most brilliant production the soul.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Or, to use another illustration: just as, when a man carries the light of a lamp into a dark place the darkness is forthwith put to flight and makes no appearance; so we ought to understand that, on the soul’s introduction into the body the darkness is straightway banished, and one nature at once effected, and one man constituted in one species.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And thus, agreeably therewith, it will be allowed that the new wine is put into new bottles, and that the piece of new cloth is not put into the old garment.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But from this we are able to show that there is a unison of powers in these two substances, that is to say, in that of the body and in that of the soul; of which unison that greatest teacher in the Scriptures, Paul, speaks, when he tells us, that “God hath set the members every one of them in the body as it hath pleased Him.”
